title: An Upper Bound on the Theoretical Activity Coefficient of Non-Electrolytes
abstract:
952
¹ 2002 WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH & Co. KGaA, Weinheim
1439-4235/02/03/11 $ 20.00+.50/0
CHEMPHYSCHEM 2002, 3, 952 ±956
An Upper Bound on the Theoretical Activity
Coefficient of Non-Electrolytes
Abraham Marmur*[a]
An upper bound on the activity coefficient, calculated from its
theoretical definition, is formulated and discussed in detail for
binary solutions of non-electrolytes. It is compared with a previous
order-of-magnitude analysis that predicted it to be e. The existence
of an upper bound points to a major discrepancy between
theoretical and actual (experimentally measured) activity coeffi-
cients in highly nonideal solutions: The actual activity coefficient
may be much higher than the theoretical upper bound. It is shown
that the theoretical activity coefficient of the more volatile
component must indeed be much smaller than e for slightly
soluble components. For the less volatile component, the upper
bound on the theoretical activity coefficient is shown to be
approximately equal to the relative volatility. However, it is
demonstrated that this upper bound may be grossly exaggerated
when the relative volatility is high. In addition, as shown for
aqueous solutions of n-alkanes that are less volatile than water,
even when the upper bound is much higher than e, it may still be
much smaller than the actual activity coefficient.
KEYWORDS:
activity coefficients ¥ hydrophobic effect ¥ nonideal solutions
¥ partial molar volumes ¥ solubility
Low solubility of liquids and gases in liquids is essential for the
existence of many systems and processes. For example, the low
solubility of air in water underlies the existence of our
atmosphere, and the low solubility of lipids in water is the most
fundamental property that determines biology as we know it.
Chemical engineering and chemistry examples include a variety
of separation processes that depend on limited solubility in
general, and on the special solubility properties of surfactants in
particular. On the other hand, many environmental problems are
caused by the immiscibility of organic liquids in water. Thus,
understanding low solubility is a vital issue.
The quantitative key to low solubility, which actually is an
extreme case of solution nonideality, is the value of the activity
coefficient. As is well known, the activity coefficient of a
component in an ideal solution equals one, solution nonideality
is characterized by the activity coefficient being different from
one, and phase separation occurs when activity coefficients are
sufficiently larger than one. The present discussion is focused on
solutions for which the activity coefficient is larger than one,
since these are the values that may lead to limited solubility.
Therefore, the term ™solution nonideality∫within the present
context will refer only to such solutions. Textbooks on classical
thermodynamics offer Equation (1) for calculating the activity
coefficient i of component i in solution from equation-of-state
data (sometimes indirectly through the fugacity). R is the
universal gas constant, T is the absolute temperature, P is the
pressure, V≈i is the partial molar volume of component i in
solution, and Vi is the molar volume of the pure component i at
the same P and T.
lni 
1
RT
P
0
(V≈i  Vi)dP
(1)
As can be understood from the difference under the integral
in Equation (1), the standard state with which component i in
solution is compared is the pure component at the same P and T.
The activity coefficient calculated from Equation (1) will be
referred to below as the theoretical activity coefficient.
Based on an order of magnitude analysis, it was suggested
that the theoretical activity coefficient has an upper bound:
i  e.[1] Obviously, this is in contrast with higher, and even much
higher, values of activity coefficients that are derived from
experimental data (to be referred to below as actual activity
coefficients), especially for scarcely soluble components. For
example, n-octane for an aqueous solution is 107 at room
temperature.[2] This discrepancy may have important implica-
tions on understanding low solubility in general, and the
hydrophobic effect in particular, in terms of the dispersion state
of solutes. Since Equation (1) is implicitly based on the assump-
tion that all solute molecules are dispersed as single molecules,
the above discrepancy may be resolved, for example, by
assuming that solute molecules in highly nonideal solutions
are dispersed as molecular aggregates rather than single
molecules. In this case, the entropy change of mixing is much
lower than in a single-molecule dispersion, thus leading to a
higher change in the Gibbs energy of mixing, that is, to higher
activity coefficients.[1] This model is in agreement with exper-
imental evidence regarding the existence of clusters in aqueous
solutions of alcohols, carboxylic acids, and acetone (see for
[a] Prof. A. Marmur
Department of Chemical Engineering
Technion–Israel Institute of Technology
32000 Haifa (Israel)
Fax: (972)4± 829 ± 3088
E-mail: marmur@tx.technion.ac.il
 Upper Bound on the Theoretical Activity Coefficient of Non-Electrolytes
CHEMPHYSCHEM 2002, 3, 952± 956
953
example refs. [3 ± 14]) and with a recent molecular dynamics
study of hydrocarbon molecules in water.[15] Therefore, the
corroboration of the existence of an upper bound on i is a
fundamentally important issue.
It is very difficult to demonstrate this upper bound by exact
calculations, partly because of the uncertainty associated with
the calculation of partial molar volumes in the two-phase region.
However, it is possible to make more detailed estimates of the
theoretical activity coefficient for various situations. This is done
in the present paper by developing more specific upper bounds
that depend on the relative volatility of the components and the
extent of nonideality of the solution. One of the tools needed to
achieve this goal is an equation for the partial molar volume for a
two-phase system; this equation is also developed below.
Theory
The present analysis is concerned with nonideal solutions of
non-electrolytes, at moderate pressures and temperatures, far
from solution critical points. As explained above, only solutions
for which i  1 are considered. For simplicity, only binary
solutions are discussed. The central point in Equation (1) is the
difference in molar volume between the solute in solution and
the solute as a pure component. Therefore, in discussing the
calculation of lni at a given temperature, it is useful to
distinguish between three pressure ranges: Pressures that are
sufficiently low for both the solution and the pure solute to be in
the gas state; intermediate pressures for which the solution and
the pure solute are in different states; and sufficiently high
pressures for which both the solution and the pure solute are in
the liquid state.
The contribution of the latter range to lni through the
integral in Equation (1) may be positive or negative, depending
on the sign of V≈i Vi. It is, however, negligible under moderate
pressures and temperatures, even when V≈i Vi is positive. This is
so for the following reasons: a) V≈i Vi should be less than a
typical molar volume of a liquid (104 m3mol1), since it seems
reasonable that the partial molar volume in the liquid state
cannot be more than twice that of the pure component; b) the
integration is carried over a pressure difference of less than an
order of magnitude of 106 Pa when moderate pressures are
considered; and c) the value of RT at room temperature is
2.5  103 Jmol1. Taking these observations into account, the
contribution of the liquid state to lni would be, at most, 4 
102 at moderate pressures and temperatures, far below the
proposed upper bound of 1.
A similar conclusion (for different reasons, though) can be
reached for the low-pressure range. For relatively low pressures,
the volume calculated for many real gases at or above room
temperature is very close to that of an ideal gas. Quantitatively, it
can be shown that this assumption holds for P/Pc2(T/Tc)2,
based on the van der Waals equation of state that is quite
sufficient for such order of magnitude calculations. In this
expression, Pc is the critical pressure and Tc is the critical
temperature. For many substances, Tc is in the range of hundreds
of kelvin (usually less than 700 K) and Pc a few megapascals.
Therefore, for pressures less than about 0.5 MPa, at or above
room temperature, the ideal gas assumption usually works well.
Consequently, under these conditions, the partial molar volume
in the gas state is very close to the molar volume of the pure
component, and the contribution to the theoretical activity
coefficient is negligible.
In the intermediate pressure range (IPR), where the solution
and the pure solute are in different states, the value of V≈i  Vi
may be meaningful. Therefore, this pressure range needs to be
discussed in detail. Within part of this pressure range the
solution exists as a two-phase system. This presents a funda-
mental problem, since it is not clear how to calculate the partial
molar volume for a component in a two-phase system. There is a
need, therefore, to define partial molar volumes for a two-phase
system in a fundamental way. This is accomplished by the
following. The molar volume V of a two-phase system can be
expressed in terms of the molar volumes, V l and V v, of the liquid
and vapor phases by Equation (2), where nl and nv are the total
numbers of moles in the liquid and vapor phases, respectively.

The relationship between the numbers of moles in the two
phases can be expressed in terms of the mole fractions of
component i, according to the well-known lever rule that is
derived from a component mass balance, Equations (3) and (4).

Equation (2), one obtains Equation (5).

may be meaningful. Therefore, this pressure range needs to be
discussed in detail. Within part of this pressure range the
solution exists as a two-phase system. This presents a funda-

molar volume for a component in a two-phase system. There is a
need, therefore, to define partial molar volumes for a two-phase
system in a fundamental way. This is accomplished by the
following. The molar volume V of a two-phase system can be
expressed in terms of the molar volumes, V l and V v, of the liquid
and vapor phases by Equation (2), where nl and nv are the total
numbers of moles in the liquid and vapor phases, respectively.
The relationship between the numbers of moles in the two
phases can be expressed in terms of the mole fractions of
component i, according to the well-known lever rule that is
derived from a component mass balance, Equations (3) and (4).
In these equations, xi, yi, and zi are the mole fractions of
component i in the liquid phase, vapor phase, and in the whole
system, respectively. On substituting Equations (3) and (4) into
Equation (2), one obtains Equation (5).
By definition, the partial molar volumes for the liquid phase,
for the vapor phase, and for the whole system are given by
Equations (6) ± (8), where V≈l
i are the partial molar volumes
of i in the liquid and vapor phases, respectively, and V≈i is defined
as the partial molar volume of i in the whole system.
Substituting Equations (6) ± (8) into Equation (5), and compar-
ison of terms for a given component i (taking into account that
Equation (5) holds for any component) leads to Equation (9).

It is easy to verify that when only one phase exists (either xi  zi
or yi  zi) the partial molar volume of i in the system turns into
the partial molar volume for that phase. Also, it will be useful to
note that each coefficient in Equation (9) is a fraction, Equa-
tions (10) and (11), and that the sum of the coefficients is given
Thus, the partial molar volume of i in the system is a weighted
average of the partial molar volumes of i in the liquid and vapor
phases. This fact is essential for discussing bounds on the activity
coefficient, as explained below.
Equilibrium phase diagrams of nonideal systems may be quite
complex, especially for immiscible liquids. Therefore, to demon-
strate the principal arguments of estimating the activity
coefficient, the discussion is first focused on a relatively simple
phase diagram, as shown in Figure 1. More complex situations

When the solute is the more volatile component (i 2),
situations exist, as shown in Figures 2 and 3, in which the
contribution of the IPR is not negative. These situations occur

It should be noted that when the solute concentration is very

theoretical activity coefficient is negative. An important case of
low solubility of the more volatile component is that of gases
above their critical temperature. In this case, the phase
equilibrium loop shown in Figure 1 does not extend all the
way to x1 0, nevertheless the reasoning given above for the

From the above theoretical considerations, it is clear that the
only meaningful contribution to the theoretical activity coef-
ficient can stem from the IPR, and that the upper bound on the
theoretical activity coefficient depends on the relative volatility
of the component. For the more volatile component (i  2), the
contribution of the IPR to the theoretical activity coefficient is
negative, except for mole fraction ranges for which PH happens
2. This is not the case for very low
concentrations, which are of main interest when low solubility
is discussed. Thus, for most cases of low solubility 2 e, in
agreement with the previous order-of-magnitude analysis.[1]
Prominent examples of interest include highly nonideal solu-
tions of a gas in a liquid, such as aqueous solutions of the low
alkanes (up to n-butane at 300 K), or oxygen, or nitrogen.
Examples of highly nonideal solutions of a more volatile liquid in
another liquid include aqueous solutions at 300 K of n-pentane
through n-heptane, or of 1- or 2-butanol (all vapor pressures that
are referred to in this paper are calculated from the Antoine
equation[16]). In all these cases, the actual (experimental) activity
coefficient is much higher than e, as can easily be deduced from
the low solubilities of these materials in water. For a slightly
soluble component, the actual activity coefficient is given by the
reciprocal of its saturation mole fraction, xs. Therefore, whenever
xs  1/e  0.368, the corresponding actual activity coefficients is
higher than e. Clearly, all the above examples conform to this
condition. Thus, a major discrepancy exists between the
theoretical and actual activity coefficients in the case of a
slightly soluble solute that is more volatile than the solvent.
The situation is different with respect to the less volatile
component. The upper bound on the theoretical activity
coefficient is given by Equation (14), which in most cases of
low solubility (since then PH(z1 0)  Ps
This means that 1 e if the relative volatility is smaller than e.
Examples include aqueous solutions at 300 K of n-octane,
1-pentanol, or 1-hexanol. Again, it is clear that the corresponding
actual activity coefficients are much higher than e, based on the
low solubilities of these components in water. It is obvious that
in these cases, too, a discrepancy of a few orders of magnitude
exists between the theoretical and actual activity coefficients.
When the relative volatility is higher than e, the situation is less
obvious. From a qualitative physical ± chemical point of view, it
does not seem reasonable that, for example, n-decane (which is
less volatile than water at 300 K with a volatility ratio of 17.8 e)
will behave differently in water than n-heptane (which is more
volatile than water at 300 K) or n-octane (volatility ratio at 300 K
of 1.7 e). The question, then, is whether the upper bound given
by Equations (14) or (16) is realistic, that is, whether it is not too
high. It may be too high if the partial molar volume is much
smaller than the ideal-gas volume at the same pressure and
temperature. As proven above, the partial molar volume of the
solute, V≈i, is a weighted average of V≈v
i , must be approximately equal to the
ideal-gas molar volume, V ig. However, V≈i may be closer to V≈l
As an example for an obvious exaggeration due to Equa-
tion (16), it is possible to consider a solution of two liquid alkanes
as different in vapor pressures as possible, such as n-eicosane in
n-heptane at 60C. Their vapor pressures are 4.93  105 and
28.1 kPa, respectively. In this case, Equation (16) would lead to an
CHEMPHYSCHEM 2002, 3, 952 ±956
upper bound on lni of 13.3, which is extremely high (almost as
high as that of n-octane in water). This is obviously an
exaggerated estimate, since n-eicosane should be much more
soluble in n-heptane than n-octane is in water. The reason for
this exaggeration is that when the difference in vapor pressures
is so large, a significant part of the integration in Equation (1)
crosses the two-phase region, PL  P   PH, and that within a
meaningful part of this pressure range V≈i is close to V≈l
Thus, Equations (14) or (16) should be used with discretion.
They indeed yield an absolute upper bound on the contribution
of the IPR to lni, but this upper bound may be very
exaggerated. For high relative volatilities, the upper bound
given by Equation (14) or (16), though exaggerated, is still useful
when it turns out to be much lower than the actual activity
coefficient. In such cases, though it is higher than e, it never-
theless points to the discrepancy between the theoretical and
actual activity coefficient. Figure 4 demonstrates this point for
Figure 4. The natural logarithms of the approximate actual activity coefficients
(- - - -) and upper bounds on the theoretical activity coefficients (––), which are
equal to the relative volatilities, versus the number of carbon atoms for n-alkanes
aqueous solutions of n-alkanes at 300 K, by showing the natural
logarithms of the actual activity coefficients and the relative
volatilities versus the number of carbon atoms in the molecule. It
is difficult to find reliable solubility data for n-alkanes longer than
n-octane from which the actual activity coefficient can be
calculated. Therefore, the correlation of Equation (17), where ac
is the actual activity coefficient for n-alkanes in water at 300 K, and
nc is the number of carbon atoms in the molecule, which was
developed for n-alkanes up to n-octane[2] is also assumed to hold
approximately, for demonstration purposes, up to n-nonadecane.
The relative volatility is calculated, as mentioned above, using
the Antoine equation.[16] Figure 4 clearly shows that even when
the (exaggerated) upper bound on the theoretical activity
coefficient is much higher than e, it is still many orders of
magnitude smaller than the actual activity coefficient. In
addition, as a curiosity, it is interesting to note that the slopes
of the two lines, which represent the addition per carbon atom,
are very similar: 1.25 for the ln line versus 1.48 from
Equation (17). This example can be generalized for slightly
soluble solutes by stating that the discrepancy between the
theoretical and actual activity coefficient definitely exists when-
In summary, the above detailed analysis corroborates the
existence of a major discrepancy between the theoretical and
actual activity coefficients for highly nonideal, binary solutions of
non-electrolytes (low solubility situations). In such solutions, the
upper bound on the theoretical activity coefficient of a solute
that is more volatile than the solvent is much lower than the
actual activity coefficient. The situation appears to be less
obvious when the solute is less volatile than the solvent. In this
case, the best upper bound that can be formulated is the relative
volatility, which may be quite high. However, the above
discussion shows that a) the relative volatility may be a grossly
exaggerated upper bound when it is high, and b) the discrep-
ancy exists even for high values of the relative volatility, when
they are still much lower than the actual activity coefficient, as in
the case of aqueous solutions of alkanes.
